title: Human Papillomavirus Infections
authors: Darron R. Brown, Aaron C. Ermel

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: HPV Pathogenesis: Role of E6 and E7 Proteins in Malignant Transformation
items.content: The early proteins E6 and E7 are crucial for HPV's ability to cause malignant transformation. They target key cell-cycle regulatory molecules, p53 and Rb (retinoblastoma protein), leading to their degradation.
keyPoints: E6 targets p53 for degradation
E7 targets Rb for degradation
These actions are responsible for the malignant transformation associated with high-risk HPV types
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: HPV Tissue Tropism and Replication Cycle
items.content: HPV is a tissue-tropic virus that infects basal keratinocytes in specific anatomic sites following microtrauma. The replication cycle completes as these infected cells differentiate, with virions assembling in the nuclei of differentiated keratinocytes.
keyPoints: HPV targets basal keratinocytes
Infection occurs after microtrauma exposes these cells to the virus
Virion assembly occurs in the nuclei of differentiated keratinocytes
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Immune Response and HPV Infection Control
items.content: HPV infection does not have a viremic phase, leading to an incomplete antibody response. However, cell-mediated immunity, particularly CD4+ T cells, plays a crucial role in controlling HPV progression.
keyPoints: No viremic phase in HPV infection
60–70% of individuals develop detectable serum antibodies
CD4+ T cell regulation is critical for controlling HPV infections
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.title: HPV Infection Clearance and Reactivation Dynamics
items.content: Most HPV infections are cleared within 6 to 9 months, but can be detected again later due to viral latency and reactivation rather than reinfection.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of young women show evidence of HPV infection shortly after first coital experience.
HPV clearance typically occurs within 6 to 9 months.
Episodic detection likely indicates viral latency followed by reactivation.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.title: HPV and Cervical Cancer Epidemiology
items.content: Persistent oncogenic HPV infection is the primary risk factor for cervical cancer, with types 16 and 18 being most prevalent in causing this malignancy.
keyPoints: HPV causes nearly 100% of cervical cancers.
More than 600,000 women are diagnosed annually; over 300,000 die from invasive cervical cancer globally.
HPV types 16 and 18 cause at least 70% of cervical squamous cell carcinomas and 85% of cervical adenocarcinomas.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.title: HPV-Associated Cancers Beyond the Cervix
items.content: HPV is implicated in various cancers, including anal, vulvar, vaginal, penile, and oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas.
keyPoints: HPV types 16 and 18 cause nearly 90% of anal cancers worldwide.
HPV causes 50–70% of vulvar and vaginal cancers, 50% of penile cancers, and at least 65% of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas (OPSCCs).
Rates of OPSCC in the U.S. have increased significantly among white men aged 40-50.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.title: HIV Accelerates HPV Disease Progression and Treatment Resistance
items.content: HIV infection accelerates the natural history of HPV infections, leading to more frequent development of genital warts that are harder to treat. It is also associated with higher rates of precancerous cervical lesions such as CIN 3, which precedes cervical cancer.
keyPoints: HIV-infected individuals have a higher likelihood of developing genital warts and recalcitrant lesions.
Consistent association between HIV infection and precancerous cervical lesions including CIN 3.
Significantly higher rates of cervical cancer in women with HIV/AIDS compared to the general population.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.title: Increased Incidence of Anal Cancer in HIV-Infected Populations
items.content: HIV infection significantly increases the risk of anal cancer, especially among MSM. Despite ART, the incidence remains high due to preexisting oncogenic HPV infections.
keyPoints: Incidence of anal cancer is strongly influenced by HIV infection.
Among HIV-infected MSM, the incidence can be as high as 130 cases per 100,000 compared to 5 cases per 100,000 in HIV-negative MSM.
ART has not significantly reduced the incidence of anal cancer and high-grade anal intraepithelial neoplasia.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.title: Characteristics and Locations of Genital Warts
items.content: Genital warts, primarily caused by HPV types 6 or 11, manifest as soft sessile growths with smooth or rough surfaces. They appear in various genital locations including the male urethra, penis, scrotum, female vulva, vagina, and cervix, as well as perianal, anal, and oropharyngeal areas.
keyPoints: Genital warts are caused primarily by HPV type 6 or 11.
Penile genital warts typically measure 2–5 mm in diameter and often occur in groups.
Vulvar warts appear as soft, whitish papules with multiple fine projections, commonly found in the introitus and labia.
Clinical differentiation of vulvar or vaginal lesions from malignancy is not always reliable; biopsy may be necessary.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.title: Cervical and Anal Lesions in HPV Infection
items.content: Subclinical cervical HPV infections are common, with lesions often appearing as papillary proliferations near the transformation zone. Cervical cancer may present with symptoms like erosion and bleeding or as an exophytic mass. Anal squamous cell cancer presents variably, with 20% of patients asymptomatic at diagnosis.
keyPoints: Subclinical cervical HPV infections are common; cervix may appear normal on examination.
Cervical lesions often present as papillary proliferations near the transformation zone.
Early cervical carcinomas appear eroded and bleed easily, while advanced ones can be ulcerated or exophytic.
Anal cancer presentations include rectal bleeding, pain, or a mass sensation; 20% of patients may be asymptomatic at diagnosis.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Behavioral Strategies to Reduce HPV Exposure
items.content: Abstinence from sexual activity may reduce the risk of HPV infection, as studies show a correlation between the number of lifetime sexual partners and HPV detection rates. Nonpenetrative sexual activities can also transmit HPV.
keyPoints: HPV is transmitted through direct contact with infected genital skin or mucosal surfaces.
Abstinence correlates with lower HPV detection rates.
Nonpenetrative sex may still pose a risk for HPV transmission.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Condom Use in Reducing HPV Risk
items.content: Latex condoms can reduce the risk of HPV infection and associated diseases, though they are not a substitute for cervical cancer screening or vaccination.
keyPoints: Correct and consistent condom use is partially effective against HPV.
Condoms have been linked to regression of CIN in women and penile lesions in men.
Condoms should complement, not replace, other preventive measures like vaccination.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Efficacy of Bivalent HPV Vaccine (Cervarix)
items.content: The bivalent vaccine Cervarix is highly effective against persistent infections and high-grade cervical lesions caused by HPV types 16 and 18.
keyPoints: Vaccine efficacy: 94.9% against CIN 2 or worse, 91.7% against CIN 3 or worse, and 100% against AIS for HPV 16/18.
Approved for females aged 9–25 years in the U.S., though not currently marketed there.
Common adverse events include injection-site pain, redness, swelling, fatigue, headache, and myalgia.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Safety Profile and Approval of Quadrivalent HPV Vaccine
items.content: The quadrivalent HPV vaccine has demonstrated a high safety profile across multiple clinical trials involving nearly 12,000 women aged 9–26 years. It is approved for preventing genital warts and cervical cancer caused by HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18 in females and males aged 9–26, as well as anal cancer and associated lesions.
keyPoints: Safety data from at least seven clinical trials with nearly 12,000 women aged 9–26 years.
No deaths considered vaccine-related; similar serious adverse events between vaccine and placebo groups.
Approved for preventing genital warts and cervical cancer in females and males aged 9–26 due to HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18.
Also approved for prevention of anal cancer and associated lesions.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Efficacy and Safety of Nine-Valent HPV Vaccine
items.content: The nine-valent HPV vaccine, Gardasil-9, targets six additional oncogenic HPV types beyond those covered by the quadrivalent vaccine. It has shown a noninferior antibody response to HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18 in females aged 16–26 years and is highly effective against high-grade cervical, vulvar, or vaginal disease caused by five additional oncogenic HPV types.
keyPoints: Approved for males and females aged 9–45; targets HPV types 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, and 58.
Noninferior antibody response to HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18 compared with the quadrivalent vaccine in females aged 16–26 years.
Vaccine efficacy against high-grade cervical, vulvar, or vaginal disease caused by additional oncogenic HPV types is 96.7% (95% CI, 80.9–99.8%).
Safe and extremely well tolerated.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Vaccination Schedule for Nine-Valent HPV Vaccine
items.content: The nine-valent HPV vaccine is administered intramuscularly with a two-dose schedule for individuals aged 9 to 15 and a three-dose schedule for those starting on or after their fifteenth birthday, including persons with certain immunocompromising conditions.
keyPoints: Two doses at months 0 and 6 for ages 9–14; three doses at months 0, 2, and 6 for ages 15–45.
Three doses recommended for those starting on or after the fifteenth birthday and for persons with immunocompromising conditions.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Cross-Protection and Efficacy of HPV Vaccines
items.content: HPV vaccines provide cross-protection against virus types closely related to HPV 16 or 18. However, efficacy varies between vaccine types over time, with the bivalent vaccine showing more initial protection against certain nonvaccine types but waning for some.
keyPoints: Cross-protection is likely shorter-lived than direct efficacy against vaccine types.
Bivalent vaccine shows higher short-term efficacy against HPV 31, 33, and 45 compared to the quadrivalent vaccine.
Efficacy against persistent infections with HPV 31 and 45 wanes over time in bivalent vaccine trials.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Single-Dose Vaccination Strategy
items.content: The WHO recommends a single-dose HPV vaccination for cost savings, yielding similar antibody titers to two doses. However, the level and duration of protection are not fully understood.
keyPoints: WHO recommends single-dose vaccination for 9-15-year-olds as a cost-saving measure.
Antibody levels from single-dose strategy appear similar to those from a two-dose regimen.
The effectiveness and duration of protection with a single dose are not fully understood.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: HPV Vaccination Recommendations for Adults
items.content: For adults aged 27-45, the ACIP recommends shared clinical decision-making regarding HPV vaccination due to lower expected benefits and prior exposure risks.
keyPoints: Catch-up vaccination is recommended through age 26; not routinely beyond that.
Shared decision-making for those 27-45 years old considers individual risk factors like new sexual partners.
Vaccine effectiveness decreases with age due to likely previous HPV exposures.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: HPV Vaccination in PLWH: Efficacy and Recommendations
items.content: HPV vaccines are prophylactic and safe for people living with HIV (PLWH), generating high antibody levels, especially in those with higher CD4 counts and lower viral loads. However, efficacy is limited in adults over 27 years due to pre-existing infections.
keyPoints: HPV vaccines prevent new infections but not progression of established ones.
Vaccination generates high antibody levels, though generally lower than in HIV-uninfected individuals.
Efficacy trial for quadrivalent HPV vaccine in adults over 27 with HIV showed no efficacy due to pre-existing infections at baseline.
HPV vaccination is recommended for PLWH aged 11–26 years (three doses).
Limited benefit and minimal public health impact of vaccinating PLWH older than 26 years.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: HPV Vaccination Decision-Making for Older PLWH
items.content: For PLWH aged 27–45, vaccination may not be fully beneficial due to pre-existing infections. However, shared clinical decision-making is advised in cases where potential benefits might exist.
keyPoints: Current data do not strongly support HPV vaccination for PLWH older than 26 years.
Potential vaccine benefit exists in specific situations despite limited efficacy.
Shared clinical decision-making between provider and patient is recommended for PLWH aged 27–45.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.title: Initiation and Frequency of Cervical Cancer Screening
items.content: The American Society of Colposcopy and Cervical Pathology (ASCCP) guidelines recommend initiating cervical cancer screening at age 21, regardless of sexual debut. Women aged 21–29 should have a Pap smear every 3 years if results are normal. For women aged 30–65, the interval can be extended to every 5 years with negative HPV DNA co-testing.
keyPoints: Initiate screening at age 21
Pap smear every 3 years for ages 21-29 if normal
Extend to every 5 years for ages 30-65 with negative HPV DNA co-test
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.title: HPV DNA Testing as Primary Screening
items.content: In the U.S., three FDA-approved assays are available for primary screening using HPV DNA testing. Positive results for HPV types 16 or 18 warrant colposcopy, while detection of other high-risk types requires cytology.
keyPoints: FDA-approved assays: cobas HPV Test, BD Onclarity HPV Assay, Alinity m HR HPV Assay
Positive for HPV 16/18 indicates need for colposcopy
Other high-risk types detected require cytology
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.title: Screening Guidelines for HIV-Infected Women
items.content: For women ≤30 years with HIV, cervical cytology is preferred. Screening should begin within 1 year of HIV diagnosis. If the first Pap smear is normal, subsequent tests are annual until three negatives are achieved, then every 3 years.
keyPoints: Cervical cytology preferred for women ≤30 with HIV
Begin screening within 1 year of HIV diagnosis
Annual Pap smears until three negative results, then every 3 years
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.title: Screening for Anal Cancer in High-Risk Populations
items.content: There are no established guidelines from the United States Preventative Services Task Force for anal cancer screening. However, consensus guidelines exist for high-risk groups such as those with HIV. Current HIV treatment guidelines suggest potential benefits of screening, though consistent evidence on reducing morbidity and mortality is lacking.
keyPoints: No endorsed anal cancer screening guidelines by USPSTF
Consensus guidelines for high-risk populations (e.g., HIV)
Potential benefit in screening noted but not consistently demonstrated
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.title: HPV Vaccination for Prevention of Anal and Head/Neck Cancers
items.content: The incidence of HPV-associated head and neck cancers has surpassed cervical cancer in the US as of 2020. While no screening guidelines exist, HPV vaccination is anticipated to be effective against both anal and head and neck cancers associated with HPV.
keyPoints: HPV-associated head and neck cancer incidence surpasses cervical cancer
No established screening guidelines for these cancers
HPV vaccination expected to prevent anal and head/neck cancers
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.title: Imiquimod Use in Genital Warts Treatment
items.content: Imiquimod, a topical immunomodulatory agent, is used for treating genital warts. The 5% cream formulation is applied three times weekly at bedtime for up to 16 weeks, clearing warts in approximately 56% of patients, more often in women than men. Recurrence rates are about 13%, with common local inflammatory side effects.
keyPoints: Imiquimod 5% cream used for genital warts
Applied three times weekly for up to 16 weeks
~56% clearance rate (higher in women)
Recurrence rate ~13%
Common local inflammatory side effects
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which proteins are responsible for the malignant transformation of cells infected with HPV?
items.explanation: The E6 and E7 proteins target p53 and Rb for degradation, leading to malignant transformation.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What is the role of HPV types 16 and 18 in cervical cancer?
options: They are low-risk for cervical cancer
They cause dysplastic lesions and a high percentage of invasive cancers
They have no association with cervical cancer
items.explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 are considered high risk because they cause dysplastic lesions and a high percentage of invasive cervical cancers.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: How is HPV primarily transmitted between individuals?
options: Via contact with virus-containing desquamated keratinocytes
items.explanation: HPV is transmitted by contact with virus contained in desquamated keratinocytes from an infected individual.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What percentage of individuals develop a detectable serum antibody response to natural HPV infection?
items.explanation: Natural HPV infection of the genital tract gives rise to a detectable serum antibody response in 60–70% of individuals.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.question: Which HPV types have been concluded by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) to be carcinogenic in the uterine cervix?
items.explanation: The IARC has concluded that HPV types 16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58, and 59 are carcinogenic in the uterine cervix.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.question: What is the most significant risk factor for cervical cancer?
items.explanation: Persistent HPV infection is identified as the most significant risk factor for cervical cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.question: What percentage of cervical cancers worldwide are caused by HPV types 16 and 18?
items.explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 cause at least 70% of cervical squamous cell carcinomas worldwide.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.question: What is the approximate percentage of women who develop cervical cancer after an oncogenic HPV infection?
items.explanation: Only approximately 3–5% of women with an oncogenic HPV infection will develop cervical cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.question: Which factor is consistently associated with precancerous cervical lesions in HIV-infected individuals?
items.explanation: A direct relationship exists between low CD4+ T lymphocyte count and the risk of cervical cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.question: What has been the impact of ART on the incidence of cervical cancer in HIV-infected women?
items.explanation: The incidence of cervical cancer has not changed significantly since ART was introduced.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.question: What is the expected trend for HPV-associated cancers in HIV-infected patients due to ART?
options: Decrease due to improved immune function
Increase due to prolonged life expectancies
Remain unchanged as ART has no effect on cancer incidence
Decrease due to reduced HPV infections
items.explanation: The burden of HPV-associated cancers is expected to increase due to prolonged life expectancies provided with ART.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.question: Which HPV types are primarily responsible for causing genital warts?
items.explanation: Genital warts are primarily caused by HPV types 6 or 11.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.question: Where are vulvar lesions most often located?
items.explanation: Vulvar lesions are most often located in the introitus and labia.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.question: What is a common presentation of early cervical carcinomas caused by HPV infection?
options: Papillary proliferations near the transformation zone
items.explanation: Early cervical carcinomas appear eroded and bleed easily.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following behaviors is associated with a reduced risk of HPV infection?
items.explanation: Abstinence may reduce HPV infections as people with no history of sexual intercourse have a lower detection rate of HPV.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the efficacy of the bivalent HPV vaccine against CIN 2 or worse due to HPV types 16 and 18?
items.explanation: The bivalent HPV vaccine has a vaccine efficacy of 94.9% against CIN 2 or worse due to HPV types 16 and 18.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which HPV vaccine is marketed under the name Gardasil?
items.explanation: The quadrivalent L1 VLP vaccine (HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18) is marketed under the name Gardasil.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What type of adverse events were more frequently reported in the bivalent vaccine group compared to the control group?
items.explanation: Injection-site adverse events (pain, redness, swelling) and systemic adverse events (fatigue, headache, myalgia) were reported more frequently in the HPV vaccine group.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which adverse events were reported at higher rates with Gardasil compared to other vaccines?
items.explanation: Rates of syncope and venous thrombotic events were higher with Gardasil than those usually observed with other vaccines.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended dosing schedule for the nine-valent HPV vaccine in individuals starting on or after their fifteenth birthday?
items.explanation: For individuals starting on or after their fifteenth birthday, the nine-valent HPV vaccine is administered three times (months 0, 2, and 6).
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which additional oncogenic HPV types does Gardasil-9 target beyond those in the quadrivalent vaccine?
items.explanation: Gardasil-9 targets HPV types 31, 33, 45, 52, and 58 in addition to those targeted by the quadrivalent vaccine.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the estimated global protection level against all HPV-associated squamous cell cancers provided by Gardasil-9?
items.explanation: Mathematical models estimate that the nine-valent HPV vaccine could raise protection against all HPV-associated squamous cell cancers worldwide to at least 90%.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which HPV vaccine types are indicated to maintain stable efficacy against persistent infection over time?
items.explanation: Efficacy against persistent infection with HPV type 16 or 18 remained stable over time.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is the recommended age range for initiating HPV vaccination according to ACIP guidelines?
items.explanation: ACIP recommends HPV vaccination at age 11 or 12, but it can be initiated as early as 9 years of age.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is the ACIP's stance on prevaccination testing for HPV vaccination?
options: It is recommended to determine vaccine eligibility.
No prevaccination testing of any kind is recommended.
Testing is required only for individuals over 26 years old.
Prevaccination testing is optional based on physician discretion.
items.explanation: No prevaccination testing of any kind is recommended to establish whether the HPV vaccine should be administered.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which statement best describes the ACIP's recommendation for HPV vaccination in adults aged 27–45?
options: Vaccination should be avoided due to high risk of adverse effects.
Vaccination is only recommended if antibody testing shows susceptibility.
items.explanation: ACIP recommends shared clinical decision-making regarding HPV vaccination for adults aged 27–45 who are not adequately vaccinated.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which statement accurately describes the utility of HPV vaccines in people living with HIV?
options: HPV vaccines are effective in treating established HPV infections.
HPV vaccines prevent new HPV infections but do not treat existing ones.
HPV vaccines are contraindicated for people living with HIV.
HPV vaccines generate lower antibody levels only in those who are HIV-uninfected.
items.explanation: HPV vaccines prevent new HPV infections but do not treat existing ones, nor are they contraindicated for PLWH.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: What is the recommended number of HPV vaccine doses for girls and boys with HIV infection aged 11–26 years?
items.explanation: HPV vaccination is recommended as three doses for girls and boys with HIV infection aged 11–26 years.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which factor influences the strength of immune responses to HPV vaccines in people living with HIV?
options: The presence of high-grade dysplastic lesions
Low CD4 counts and high HIV viral loads
High CD4 counts and low HIV viral loads
items.explanation: Immune responses to HPV vaccines are stronger among PLWH with the highest CD4 counts and lowest HIV viral loads.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.question: According to the ASCCP guidelines, at what age should cervical cancer screening be initiated?
items.explanation: The ASCCP recommends initiating cervical cancer screening at age 21.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.question: For women aged 30–65 years, how often should a Pap smear be performed if HPV DNA testing is not done?
items.explanation: Women aged 30–65 should have a Pap smear every 3 years if HPV DNA testing is not performed.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.question: What is the recommended screening method for women ≤30 years of age infected with HIV?
items.explanation: For women ≤30 years of age infected with HIV, cervical cytology is the preferred method.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.question: Which HPV test types have a high enough positive predictive value to warrant colposcopy if detected?
items.explanation: A positive result for HPV type 16 or 18 has a high enough positive predictive value to warrant colposcopy.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.question: Which organization has published consensus guidelines for screening anal cancer in high-risk patients?
options: United States Preventative Services Task Force
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
items.explanation: The International Anal Neoplasia Society has published consensus guidelines regarding screening for anal cancer in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.question: What is the primary goal of treatment for HPV-associated diseases?
options: Eradication of all HPV from the body
Prevention of viral transmission and eradication of premalignant lesions
Complete elimination of symptoms without recurrence
items.explanation: The goals of treatment include prevention of viral transmission, eradication of premalignant lesions, and reduction of symptoms.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.question: Which therapeutic option is not recommended for treating vaginal, cervical, or anal lesions?
items.explanation: Imiquimod should not be used to treat vaginal, cervical, or anal lesions.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.question: What is a common side effect of imiquimod treatment for genital warts?
items.explanation: Local inflammatory side effects are common with imiquimod treatment.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: HPV has a viremic phase during infection.
items.explanation: Unlike many viral infections, HPV infection does not have a viremic phase.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: CD4+ T cell regulation is crucial in controlling HPV infections.
items.explanation: CD4+ T cell regulation plays an important role, as evidenced by higher rates of infection and disease in immunosuppressed individuals.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.statement: HPV type 16 causes at least 50% of cervical cancers.
items.explanation: HPV type 16 is particularly virulent and causes at least 50% of cervical cancers.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.statement: Most HPV infections lead to malignancy.
items.explanation: Only about 3–5% of women with an oncogenic HPV infection will develop cervical cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.statement: HIV-infected individuals are less likely than others to develop genital warts.
items.explanation: HIV-infected individuals are more likely to develop genital warts.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.statement: The incidence of anal cancer among HIV-infected MSM is significantly lower than that in the general population.
items.explanation: HIV-infected MSM have a much higher rate of anal cancer compared to HIV-negative populations.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.statement: Subclinical cervical HPV infections often result in visible changes upon examination.
items.explanation: The cervix may appear normal on examination despite subclinical HPV infections.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION
items.statement: Twenty percent of patients diagnosed with anal cancer present with specific symptoms at diagnosis.
items.explanation: Twenty percent of patients may not present with any specific symptoms when diagnosed with anal cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF HPV INFECTION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: The quadrivalent HPV vaccine is effective against external genital warts.
items.explanation: The quadrivalent HPV vaccine has demonstrated a vaccine efficacy of 98.9% against external genital warts.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Cervarix is currently marketed in the United States.
items.explanation: Cervarix is not currently marketed in the United States.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: The quadrivalent HPV vaccine is still available in the United States.
items.explanation: The quadrivalent HPV vaccine is no longer available in the United States.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: Gardasil-9 has been shown to be safe and well tolerated.
items.explanation: Like the other available HPV vaccines, Gardasil-9 is safe and extremely well tolerated.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.statement: The World Health Organization recommends a single-dose HPV vaccine regimen for cost savings.
items.explanation: WHO has recommended that 9- to 15-year-olds may be vaccinated with a single dose of an HPV vaccine as a cost-saving measure.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.statement: HPV vaccines are effective in individuals who have been previously exposed to the targeted HPV types.
items.explanation: HPV vaccines are very effective in persons who have not been exposed to vaccine-type HPV before vaccination.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.statement: HPV vaccination is recommended for people living with HIV aged over 26 years.
items.explanation: Current data do not strongly support vaccination for PLWH older than 26 years, and the public health benefit is likely minimal.

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)
items.statement: HPV vaccines are safe in people living with HIV.
items.explanation: HPV vaccines are considered safe for PLWH.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.statement: HPV DNA co-testing is recommended for women aged 21–29 years with normal Pap smears.
items.explanation: HPV DNA co-testing is not recommended for women aged 21–29 years if their initial and subsequent Pap smears are normal.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.statement: Approximately 75% of women in developed countries have been screened for cervical cancer in the past 5 years.

items.statement: Imiquimod is effective in treating cervical lesions.

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.statement: HPV vaccination is likely effective for both anal and head and neck cancers.

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: HPV targets basal keratinocytes after microtrauma.
items.reason: Microtrauma allows exposure of these cells to the virus, facilitating infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: HPV replication is completed in undifferentiated keratinocytes.
items.reason: Virions are assembled in the nuclei of differentiated keratinocytes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; HPV replication is completed as keratinocytes undergo differentiation.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · Human Papillomavirus Infections | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.assertion: Persistent carriers of oncogenic HPV types are at greatest risk for high-grade cervical dysplasia and cancer.
items.reason: HPV infection may become undetectable after 6 to 9 months, known as 'clearance'.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY
items.assertion: HPV types 16 and 18 cause nearly all cervical cancers.
items.reason: HPV types 16 and 18 are responsible for at least 70% of cervical squamous cell carcinomas and 85% of cervical adenocarcinomas.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because HPV types 16 and 18 do not cause nearly all cervical cancers; they cause a significant portion but not all.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE NATURAL HISTORY OF HPV-ASSOCIATED MALIGNANCY

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
items.assertion: The incidence of cervical cancer in HIV-infected women has decreased significantly since the introduction of ART.
items.reason: ART reduces the likelihood of HPV infection and precancerous lesions.
items.explanation: Despite reduced likelihood of new infections, preexisting oncogenic HPV infections before ART initiation have kept cervical cancer incidence unchanged.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · THE EFFECTS OF HIV ON HPV-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: The bivalent HPV vaccine is approved for prevention of cervical cancer.
items.reason: It contains L1 VLPs of HPV types 16 and 18, which are associated with a significant proportion of cervical cancers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the bivalent vaccine is approved for prevention of cervical cancer.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Condom use can completely prevent HPV infection.
items.reason: Latex condoms reduce the risk of HPV-associated diseases such as genital warts and cervical precancers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because condom use cannot completely prevent HPV infection, although it reduces the risk.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: The nine-valent HPV vaccine targets more oncogenic HPV types than the quadrivalent vaccine.
items.reason: Gardasil-9 includes protection against five additional oncogenic HPV types beyond those in the quadrivalent vaccine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: The duration of protection from the quadrivalent HPV vaccine has been established.
items.reason: No evidence of waning protection was found after a three-dose series even after 10 years of follow-up.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; while no waning protection was observed, the duration has not been definitively established.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: The bivalent HPV vaccine shows waning efficacy against persistent infections with certain types over time.
items.reason: Efficacy against persistent infection with HPV type 31 and 45 waned over time in the bivalent vaccine trials.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: Antibody testing can determine immunity to specific HPV types.
items.reason: HPV vaccines are very effective in persons who have not been exposed to vaccine-type HPV before vaccination.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because antibody testing cannot determine whether a person is immune or susceptible to a specific HPV type.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · PREVENTION OF HPV INFECTION AND DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.assertion: HPV DNA testing is recommended as a primary screen for cervical cancer.
items.reason: The role of HPV DNA testing as a primary screen for cervical cancer is changing, with several assays FDA approved for this purpose.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)
items.assertion: HPV DNA co-testing is recommended for women ≤30 years of age infected with HIV.
items.reason: HPV DNA co-testing is not recommended for this group; cervical cytology is preferred instead.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states that HPV DNA co-testing is not recommended.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.assertion: Imiquimod is used for treating genital warts.
items.reason: Imiquimod activates immune cells by binding to a Toll-like receptor leading to an inflammatory response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why imiquimod is used for treating genital warts.
items.reference: Human Papillomavirus Infections · SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING FOR HPV-ASSOCIATED CANCER (part 2) | HPV-Associated Disease | THERAPEUTIC OPTIONS
items.assertion: The most effective method of screening for anal cancer has been determined.
items.reason: Data from a randomized controlled trial noted significant reduction in progression of high-grade precancerous lesions to anal cancer with treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the passage states that the most effective method of screening for anal cancer has not been determined.
